In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over not only 
how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations 
beyond the problem itself.  
 
 
c) 
Examples of not problem contingent (universal approaches) 
 
As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and 
practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because 
CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be 
able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an 
intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy 
interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA 
approach is not to act.  
 
There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international 
relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state 
preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to 
absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying 
motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal 
institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain state behaviour in general – i.e. 
they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived 
from, their choices about how to manage a s

just need to re-orient self-interest in the long run and we can address Type 4 problems. Likewise others, engaging in what 
to me appears to be some kind of circular reasoning posits that “ Altruism can be reciprocity-driven, where individuals help others because they 
feel obligated to return a favor or anticipate future benefits.” Which means they argue, as my colleague He Liuyang notes, that “Indeed, 
evolutionary perspective suggests that altruism may have evolved alongside the impulse to condemn and even punish those who act solely in their 
own self-interests.”. My point is, as elaborated in this book, that none of the underlying ontologies of the Commons, Optimization and 
Compromise schools can take us on a Type 4 journey. Efforts to stay within their own school – usually owing to methodological training are 
understandable, but they become part of the Type 4 tragedies documented in this book (Grateful to Liuyang for a very important observation).

se from the tool we cannot tell the type of problem and the means to achieve a 
problem.  Type 3 – talks about social mechanisms with an agency to defy the current institutions. Fair 
trade considers the community, social welfare in market expansion.  It is type 3.  
Article 11: Knox-Hayes, “Negotiating climate legislation: Policy path dependence and coalition 
stabilization” (DRAFT) 
Ben’s notes: 
First it does not always follow that an application or assessment of game theory or even economic 
valuation will mean the paper is Type 2. This is because the paper may be assessing whether the theory 
works to explain something, rather than assuming the theory is correct. Second, just like cost-
effectiveness analysis improved the compliance and reduce costs of Type 4 acid rain regulations 
through the development of a “cap and trade” system, an application of game theory or ACF/sabatier 
theory could be used to assess how coalitions might influence, or not,  “on the ground” outcomes such 
as 1.5/2 degrees.  Similarly, knowledge about how dialogues work a la compromise school could be 
reshaped to think about type 4 problems (which is how many have applied Sabatier/ACF) 
• 
So game theory and costs and benefits are clues could be type 2 but not always (as the code 
book discusses) 
• 
Likewise assessing coalition formation to think about effects of problem solving would be 
expected to draw on all kinds of theory of support, including game theory – but that wouldn’t 
be its ultimate interest and it owuldn’t be realying on game theory to tell us WHETHER the 
problem could be solved – only instead to understand coalition formation 
• 
Second, 
• 
the article is very clear that its results are not consistent with ahistorical motivations assumed 
by RC theory

ovides brief summaries of the cases studied.
5
For those less familiar with QCA, conceptually this may resemble a program output rather than program outcome. QCA,
however, strictly uses the term outcome for “dependent variables” and output for “independent variables.” In this article,
I keep to the QCA terminology.
6
Online Appendix C in Appendix S1 provides an overview of the codes used.
7
See online Appendix A in Appendix S1 for a calibration of the conditions and outcomes.
8
It should be noted that the variance in outcomes observed is skewed toward negative outcomes for O1 and positive out-
comes for O2. The lack of variance in outcomes observed is not expected to affect the theoretical implications of the arti-
cle (i.e. the relevance of including a diffusion of innovations perspective in studying voluntary programs), but it may
affect the generalizability of the empirically observed patterns (Payne & Williams 2005; Schneider & Wagemann 2012).
9
See further online Appendix A in Appendix S1.
10
See further online Appendix A in Appendix S1.
11
See online Appendix A in Appendix S1.
12
More concise versions of the club theory were also explored: for example, a model where all rewards were added up, as
well as models where only an individual reward or pair of rewards was included. All of these models capture less variance
in the observed outcomes than the club theory model presented here.
13
Because of space limitations, the full analyses for sufﬁcient conditions for the outcomes O1, O2, ~O1, and ~O2 are pre-
sented in online Appendix A in Appendix S1.
14
All of the cases are discussed at some length in online Appendix B in Appendix S1.
15
See further Online Appendix A in Appendix S1.
References
Ackerman F (1997) Why Do we Recycle? Markets, Value and Public Policy. Islan

ustralian Research Council (grant

16
J. van der Heijden
The diffusion network perspective
Notes
1
It should be noted here that these three contextual conditions are related and mutually reinforce each other (Choumert
et al. 2013). Higher levels of economic development, for instance, are often related to higher levels of literacy and educa-
tion, which typically indicate greater awareness of environmental problems. Higher levels of economic development are
often also related to higher levels of mandatory environmental legislation, and such legislation may make citizens further
aware of environmental problems and the need to address these; in combination, these factors are likely to contribute to
societal pressure.
2
Online Appendix A in Appendix S1 provides a step-by-step discussion of the various analyses carried out. The number
and length of the Online Appendixes in Appendix S1 may seem unusual to scholars unfamiliar with QCA. Standard
QCA practice requires the underlying data, as well as the research steps taken and the choices made, are transparent
(Ragin 2008; Rihoux & Ragin 2009).
3
The results of the QCA analyses indicate that the cases do not cluster around country characteristics. This suggests that
the contextual conditions (i.e. existing legislation and regulation, economic circumstances, and societal pressure) are
indeed sufﬁciently constant among these countries not to have a noticeable impact on program performance.
4
Online Appendix B in Appendix S1 provides brief summaries of the cases studied.
5
For those less familiar with QCA, conceptually this may resemble a program output rather than program outcome. QCA,
